description: Everything implies itself — even when we say it about everything.

guides.text: The goal is $\forall x.\; P(x) \to P(x)$. In words: for every $x$, $P(x)$ implies itself. This is the identity theorem from problem 01, generalised to all possible things. To prove a universal statement, we pick an arbitrary witness — a fresh term variable — prove the statement for that witness, then generalise.
The rule `fix` introduces a fresh term variable into the context, just like `assume` does for propositions. It gives us $\{x\} \vdash x$ — we now have an arbitrary $x$ to work with.
Now, under the assumption that we have this arbitrary $x$, assume $P(x)$ — a concrete instance of the predicate.
The two assumptions live in separate contexts: $\{x\}$ and $\{P(x)\}$. To use both, merge them with `and-intro`.
Extract $P(x)$ from the merged context using `and-elim-r`. Now both $x$ and $P(x)$ are in the context of this step.
Discharge $P(x)$ with `->-intro`. The context shrinks to just $\{x\}$ — we've proved $P(x) \to P(x)$ under the arbitrary witness $x$.

The final move: `forall-intro` generalises over $x$. It checks that $x$ appears nowhere else free in the context (freshness) and wraps the conclusion: $\forall x.\; P(x) \to P(x)$. The witness is discharged, the proof stands for all $x$.

unlocks.name: fix
unlocks.description: Introduce a fresh term variable into the context — like assume, but for terms.

unlocks.name: ∀-intro
unlocks.description: If you prove $\varphi$ for an arbitrary $x$ not appearing elsewhere, the proof holds for all $x$. Universal generalisation.
